schools.summary: Chrysostom: unlike his gentler approach to their divisions, here Paul lays about him immediately and makes the reproach as general as possible — not singling out the offender alone but charging the whole church (for tolerating it); the fornication commonly reported, openly known, and such as was not even named (tolerated/practised) among the Gentiles — a man having his father's wife (incest, a sin even the heathen abhorred); the scandal of a wickedness in the church exceeding the heathen's (the dishonour to the gospel, the church shamed), demanding discipline (the offender to be put away, the church responsible for the toleration).

themes: it is reported commonly that there is fornication among you
not so much as named among the Gentiles
one should have his father's wife
the scandal demanding discipline

schools.summary: Gill: they were puffed up — with the gifts and eloquence of their teachers, or one party against another, or in their outward flourishing condition — when they ought to have been humbled; Paul rebuking the Corinthians for being puffed up (proud, self-confident) instead of mourning over the scandal, and for not having put away the offender — the misplaced pride that gloried while the church was defiled (the glorying amid defilement), the failure to grieve over the sin (the duty to mourn over the dishonour to Christ and the defilement of the body), and the neglect of the discipline that should have removed the wicked man (true mourning issuing in the purging).

themes: ye are puffed up
have not rather mourned
the pride that gloried while the church was defiled
the neglect of discipline

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul, whatever it might cost him and however they esteemed his interference, had adjudged this case to be so flagrant as to demand the exercise of discipline; Paul declaring that, though absent in body, he was present in spirit and had already judged the case as if present — the apostolic authority exercised at a distance (by the Spirit and his apostolic office, rendering judgment on the offender though far away), the flagrant case (so clear and gross he could judge it even absent), and the calling of the church to act on the judgment (the apostle judging, the church executing the discipline).

themes: absent in body, but present in spirit
have judged already, as though I were present
the apostolic authority at a distance
the flagrant case judged

schools.summary: Gill: the act would be done 'in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ' — by his command, power, and authority, and for his glory, in whose name all such actions (this being a miraculous one) were performed; Paul directing that the discipline be done in the name of the Lord Jesus, with the church gathered together, his spirit present (concurring), and the power of the Lord Jesus — the solemn, authoritative manner of the church's act (done in Christ's name and authority, by the assembled congregation, with the apostle's concurring spirit and the power of Christ present), the corporate and weighty nature of discipline (not a private or light matter).

themes: in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ
when ye are gathered together
with the power of our Lord Jesus Christ
the solemn corporate discipline

schools.summary: Gill distinguishes this from the church's act of excommunication — 'delivering to Satan' an apostolic act, with miraculous power, giving the offender over to Satan for bodily affliction (the destruction of the flesh), whereas the putting away (v.13) was the church's act; the offender delivered to Satan for the destruction of the flesh (the bodily chastening, or the mortifying of the sinful flesh/nature), that the spirit might be saved in the day of the Lord — the severe but merciful discipline (aimed not at destruction but at repentance and final salvation, the love in the rod), the distinction of the apostolic (extraordinary, with affliction) and the church's act.

debates.question: What is 'deliver to Satan' and 'the destruction of the flesh'?

sides.position: An apostolic act giving the offender over to Satan for bodily affliction (the body chastened), with the spirit's salvation as the aim
The church's exclusion of the offender into Satan's domain (the world), aiming at the mortifying of the sinful flesh/nature and his repentance

themes: to deliver such an one unto Satan
for the destruction of the flesh
that the spirit may be saved
the severe but merciful, saving discipline

schools.summary: Gill: their glorying — in their condition, riches, ministers, or even in conniving at the sin — was not good but criminal and dangerous; Barnes: their boasting, their confidence as if they were eminent in purity; Paul reproving their glorying and warning that a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump — the danger of tolerated sin (the contagion: as a little leaven spreads through the whole lump, so one unjudged sin spreads its corrupting influence through the whole church), the necessity of purging (the offender put away, v.7, lest the leaven corrupt the whole), the church's duty to judge and purge sin for the holiness of the body.

themes: your glorying is not good
a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump
the contagion of tolerated sin
the necessity of purging

schools.summary: Gill: 'purge out the old leaven' means removing the incestuous person (the act of excommunication, the church's duty), the allusion to the Jews' strict search for leaven before the passover; Paul calling the church to purge the old leaven that they may be a new lump, as they are unleavened, for Christ our passover is sacrificed for us — the duty to remove the corrupting sin and the offender, grounded in the typology (Christ the true Passover Lamb, John 1:29, sacrificed for his people, his blood redeeming and sheltering), and the purity that befits the redeemed (as the passover was kept with unleavened bread, so the redeemed are to be pure, the holiness grounded in the sacrifice).

themes: purge out therefore the old leaven
that ye may be a new lump
Christ our passover is sacrificed for us
the purity that befits the redeemed

schools.summary: Barnes: the language drawn from the paschal feast; it does not mean literally the passover (ceased) nor particularly the Lord's Supper, but the whole Christian life — the keeping of a perpetual feast; Paul exhorting believers to keep the feast, not with the old leaven nor the leaven of malice and wickedness (sin, ill-will, vice) but with the unleavened bread of sincerity (purity of motive, single-heartedness) and truth (integrity) — the whole Christian life as a perpetual Passover feast (as Israel kept the seven-day feast of unleavened bread), kept by purging the leaven of sin and living in sincerity and truth (the marks of the holy life).

themes: let us keep the feast
not with the leaven of malice and wickedness
the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth
the whole life a perpetual Passover

schools.summary: Chrysostom: since Paul had said 'purge out the old leaven,' they might surmise they should avoid all fornicators (even those without) — for if a sinner imparts mischief to those who have not sinned, much more should one keep away from those without; under this impression Paul clarifies; Paul referring to a former letter (a now-lost earlier epistle, not all his writings preserved) in which he had instructed them not to company with fornicators — the previous instruction against keeping company with the immoral, which the Corinthians had misunderstood (over-applying it to those outside), prompting Paul's clarification (vv.10-11, the care to be rightly understood).

themes: I wrote unto you in an epistle
not to company with fornicators
the former (lost) letter
the instruction misunderstood

schools.summary: Gill: 'the fornicators of this world' are worldly, carnal men, never called out of it; Paul's prohibition was not to be understood of such, for then one must leave the world altogether; Paul clarifying that he did not mean the fornicators, covetous, extortioners, and idolaters of the world — for to avoid all such one would have to leave the world entirely (the impossibility of total avoidance) — the necessary commerce with sinners (the believer must live among the unconverted, in business, society, daily life, cannot wholly separate, John 17:15, 'not of the world but in it'), the limit of separation (not from the world's sinners but from the immoral within the church, v.11), the balance of holy yet present.

themes: not altogether with the fornicators of this world
covetous... extortioners... idolaters
for then must ye needs go out of the world
the necessary commerce with the unconverted

schools.summary: Gill: this explains the former instruction — the separation is from one 'called a brother' (a church member) who lives in such sin; Paul instructing that they not keep company with any man called a brother who is a fornicator, covetous, idolater, railer, drunkard, or extortioner — with such a one not even to eat — the discipline of the immoral church member (the believer who lives in open, persistent, unrepented sin to be shunned, refused even table fellowship), aimed at the offender's shame and repentance (2 Thessalonians 3:14, 'that he may be ashamed') and the keeping of the church pure (the leaven purged, the holiness maintained).

themes: if any man that is called a brother be a fornicator
with such an one no not to eat
the discipline of the immoral church member
that he be ashamed, the church kept pure

schools.summary: Gill: 'to judge' here is to admonish, reprove, censure, and condemn; Lightfoot discusses the phrase, the sense being that the church's judgment extends to its own members, not to outsiders; Paul asking what he (or the church) has to do to judge those without (outside the church), since their concern is to judge those within — the limit of the church's disciplinary jurisdiction (the church judges/disciplines its own members, admonishing and excluding the unrepentant, not the men of the world, who are outside its bounds and under God's judgment, v.13), the proper sphere of church discipline the church itself.

themes: what have I to do to judge them also that are without?
do not ye judge them that are within?
the church's jurisdiction over its own
the limit of disciplinary authority

schools.summary: Gill: God will judge those without in the great day; though they fall not under the church's censure, they shall not go unpunished but be called to account; Paul declaring that God judges those without (the assurance that outsiders are not unjudged, the divine reckoning at the last day), and commanding the church to put away from among themselves the wicked person — the church's act of excommunication (the removal of the unrepentant offender, the purging of the wicked from the body, the leaven cast out, the church kept pure), echoing the repeated command of Deuteronomy ('put away the evil from among you,' Deuteronomy 17:7).

themes: them that are without God judgeth
put away from among yourselves that wicked person
the church's act of excommunication
the purging of the body
